coredata.dc:title: Three-pronged attacks by hybrid nanoassemblies involving a natural product, carbon dots, and Cu2+ for synergistic HCC therapy

coredata.dc:description: Tumor microenvironment (TME) stimuli-responsive nanoassemblies are emerging as promising drug delivery systems (DDSs), which acquire controlled release by structural transformation under exogenous stimulation. However, the design of smart stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms integrated with nanomaterials to achieve complete tumor ablation remains challenging. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop TME-based stimuli-responsive DDSs to enhance drug-targeted delivery and release at tumor sites. Herein, we proposed an appealing strategy to construct fluorescence-mediated TME stimulus-responsive nanoplatforms for synergistic cancer therapy by assembling photosensitizers (PSs) carbon dots (CDs), chemotherapeutic agent ursolic acid (UA), and copper ions (Cu2+). First, UA nanoparticles (UA NPs) were prepared by self-assembly of UA, then UA NPs were assembled with CDs via hydrogen bonding force to obtain UC NPs. After combining with Cu2+, the resulting particles (named UCCu2+ NPs) exhibited quenched fluorescence and photosensitization due to the aggregation of UC NPs. Upon entering the tumor tissue, the photodynamic therapy (PDT) and the fluorescence function of UCCu2+ were recovered in response to TME stimulation. The introduction of Cu2+ triggered the charge reversal of UCCu2+ NPs, thereby promoting lysosomal escape. Furthermore, Cu2+ resulted in additional chemodynamic therapy (CDT) capacity by reacting with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as well as by consuming glutathione (GSH) in cancer cells through a redox reaction, hence magnifying intracellular oxidative stress and enhancing the therapeutic efficacy due to reactive oxygen species (ROS) therapy. In summary, UCCu2+ NPs provided an unprecedented novel approach for improving the therapeutic efficacy through the three-pronged (chemotherapy, phototherapy, and heat-reinforced CDT) attacks to achieve synergistic therapy.

head.ce:title: Three-pronged attacks by hybrid nanoassemblies involving a natural product, carbon dots, and Cu2+ for synergistic HCC therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tumor microenvironment (TME) stimuli-responsive nanoassemblies are emerging as promising drug delivery systems (DDSs), which acquire controlled release by structural transformation under exogenous stimulation. However, the design of smart stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms integrated with nanomaterials to achieve complete tumor ablation remains challenging. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop TME-based stimuli-responsive DDSs to enhance drug-targeted delivery and release at tumor sites. Herein, we proposed an appealing strategy to construct fluorescence-mediated TME stimulus-responsive nanoplatforms for synergistic cancer therapy by assembling photosensitizers (PSs) carbon dots (CDs), chemotherapeutic agent ursolic acid (UA), and copper ions (Cu2+). First, UA nanoparticles (UA NPs) were prepared by self-assembly of UA, then UA NPs were assembled with CDs via hydrogen bonding force to obtain UC NPs. After combining with Cu2+, the resulting particles (named UCCu2+ NPs) exhibited quenched fluorescence and photosensitization due to the aggregation of UC NPs. Upon entering the tumor tissue, the photodynamic therapy (PDT) and the fluorescence function of UCCu2+ were recovered in response to TME stimulation. The introduction of Cu2+ triggered the charge reversal of UCCu2+ NPs, thereby promoting lysosomal escape. Furthermore, Cu2+ resulted in additional chemodynamic therapy (CDT) capacity by reacting with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as well as by consuming glutathione (GSH) in cancer cells through a redox reaction, hence magnifying intracellular oxidative stress and enhancing the therapeutic efficacy due to reactive oxygen species (ROS) therapy. In summary, UCCu2+ NPs provided an unprecedented novel approach for improving the therapeutic efficacy through the three-pronged (chemotherapy, phototherapy, and heat-reinforced CDT) attacks to achieve synergistic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The TME provides an endogenous environment for the retention and origination of tumor cells, which features vascularization, hypoxia, biosynthesis intermediate disorder, high vascular permeability, immunosuppression, and inflammatory responses [1,2], thereby taking the leading responsibility for tumor invasion, progression, and multidrug resistance (MDR). Given the above, more attention should be paid to explore innovatively anticancer tactics based on the TME-based stimuli-responsive nanoparticles [3,4]. The prominent characteristics of the TME could contribute to efficacious targeted therapy towards cancer cells [5,6]. To overcome the challenges of an unsatisfied systemic circulation, a low targeting efficiency, poor tumor penetration, and uncontrollable drug release faced by traditional tumor-targeted DDSs, and to consequently realize targeted cancer treatment, utilizing intelligent materials with TME stimuli-responsive diagnosis and treatment capabilities has been considered one of the most ideal approaches [7,8], thereby demonstrating obvious merits in maximizing therapeutic efficacy and minimizing the adverse events. Despite extensive research on the development and preparation of diagnostics or theranostic platforms based on TME stimuli-responsive drugs, constructing a method that is operationally and conceptually simple with favorable outcomes is highly warranted.

Benefiting from the fast development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have become the most popular research objects for constructing stimulus-responsive diagnosis and treatment platforms upon the conditions of exogenous TME trigger or exogenous activation [9–11]. Among them, CDs have appeared as a kind of suitable candidate for realizing simultaneous diagnosis and treatment due to their excellent biocompatibility, easy surface functionalization, and stable fluorescence emission [12–14]. What's more, the potential laser-mediated PDT and photothermal therapy (PTT), that can destroy cancer cells through cytotoxic ROS or local hyperthermia converted from the irradiated laser [15,16]. Nevertheless, the limited particle size (2–10 nm) of CDs limits their accumulation at tumor tissue ascribed to the undesirable enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, consequently weakening the diagnosis and treatment outcomes [17]. Therefore, the exploitation of a strategy for the construction of targeted materials that is simple in operation and concept is of importance yet continued a thrilling burden with the very finite success attained.

Considering that drug synergism stands out in elegantly addressing the issues of drug resistance and inefficient therapy, where equivalent therapeutic effect can be obtained with a much lower dose [18,19]. In that case, we wondered whether this strategy is also applicable to other therapeutic modalities [20,21]. That is to say, what happens when multiple substances were introduced into tumor cells simultaneously? Is it feasible to obtain synergistic therapeutic effects at lower drug concentrations? Notably, the UA is a representative natural hepatoprotective triterpenoid nanomaterial with acknowledged antitumor activity [22]. Our previous studies have demonstrated that UA has good anti-hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) effect with excellent biosafety, and is also considered to be an effective “green” template to self-assemble with small molecules for the construction of carrier-free nano-systems [23,24]. For instance, Zhang et al. successfully constructed a novel “core–shell” co-assembled carrier-free nano-system using UA, polyphenol (EGCG), and the EpCAM-aptamer as precursors for HCC synergistic therapy. Interestingly, the results showed a 70% inhabitation rate towards HCC [25]. Therefore, combining UA and CDs to achieve precise cancer treatment has become a positive therapeutic strategy. Furthermore, utilizing metal-ion-containing nanoparticles for stimuli-responsive anticancer therapy has become an attractive area due to their ionic interference character [26]. Particularly, Cu2+ as one of the most affluent metal ions in biosystem possesses enormous potentials in CDT ascribed to the Cu-catalyzed Fenton-like reaction induced by overexpressed H2O2 in tumor tissues [27,28]. Most importantly, previous studies have shown that Cu2+ ions can deplete supernormal GSH through a redox reaction [29,30]. Since the GSH scavenges generated ROS and consequently reduces the therapeutic outcomes of phototherapy and CDT [31,32], the GSH depletion capacity of Cu2+ will further aggravate the damage of pathological sites and amplify the therapeutic outcomes.

Given the above, the implementation of a “three-pronged attack” for synergistic cancer therapy is crucial. Given the role of drug synergism in tumor ablation and overcoming MDR, in this study, universal nanoassemblies (UCCu2+ NPs) with TME stimuli-responsive and GSH consume abilities were fabricated (Scheme 1
A) through the assembling of UA NPs and CDs, after Cu2+ was connected by electrostatic interaction. The fabricated result can be entrusted with significant attributes: Firstly, when compared with the CDs alone, the average diameter of UCCu2+ NPs was significantly increased, which was conducive to its accumulation in tumor sites due to EPR effects. Secondly, the fluorescence of UCCu2+ NPs in the neutral state was “turned off”. However, their fluorescence and phototherapy functions could be selectively “turned on” in the TME due to the lower pH and overexpressed H2O2 and supernormal GSH, thereby minimizing the side effects in healthy tissue. Third, Cu2 + in the nanoassemblies not only reacts with endogenous H2O2 to produce cytotoxic hydroxyl radical (–OH), but also significantly consumes GSH while triggering the disassembly of the nanoassemblies, and thereby amplifies oxidative stress and enhances therapeutic effects. More importantly, UCCu2+ NPs undergo disassembly upon TME stimulation, which promotes deep penetration and cell internalization and ultimately eliminates from the body. Furthermore, UCCu2+ NPs demonstrated enhanced lysosome escape compared to the UC NPs, and last but not least, the recovered photosensitization functions further improves the treatment outcomes upon laser irradiation by drug synergism (Scheme 1 B). Taken together, the above consequences were verified by both in in vivo and in vitro studies. This approach is ingenuous in operation and concept, and furnishes a revolutionary method and renewed consciousness of improving the curative effect through a synergistic therapy that used a “three-pronged attack”.

UA (98%), GSH (99.7%), H2O2, citric acid (99.5%), ethylenediamine (98%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (99.9%), 3-diphenyliso-benzofuran (DPBF) (97%) and methylene blue (MB) (98.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Hoechst 33,342 was available from Aisizhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China) (98%). GSH Assay Kit was purchased from Wanlei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) (99%). Cupric chloride (CuCl2) (99%) used in this work was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Calcein-AM/PI double stain kit (99.9%) and MTT [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] was available from Beijing Baiaolaibo Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

For the preparation of UA NPs, 50 µL of UA (4 mg/mL) was added dropwise into 950 µL of ultrapure water and vortex for 20 s to induce UA NPs (200 µg/mL) formation for subsequent use. Other formulations of UA (4 mg/mL) with different mass contents (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, and 200 μL) were also prepared consistently. The diameter and zeta potential of the NPs can be fine-tuned by adjusting the concentration of the drug and the solvent used.

Next, a 5 µL CDs solution of 4 mg/mL synthesized according to our previously reported method [24] was added to 50 µL of UA NPs by swirling in drops at a mass ratio of 10:1 (UA NPs: CDs). UC NPs were eventually acquired and subjected to characterization methods. Other NPs fabricated using the same formula and different mass ratios of UA NPs to CDs were also explicated using the same procedure. The diameter and zeta potential of the obtained UC NPs can be modulated by regulating other parameters.

To fabricate the Cu2+ induced UCCu2+ NPs, the concentrations of Cu2+ and UC NPs were screened and optimized to be 0.5 mM and 200 µg/mL, respectively. Briefly, 100 µL CuCl2 (5 mM) aqueous solution was slowly added into 900 µL of (0.2 mg/mL) UC NPs (at an equivalent concentration of UA NPs) solution. Thereafter, the mixture was kept at room temperature for 3 min under gentle stirring. After washing by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 5 min) three times, the final product was collected through centrifugation.

SEM measurements were conducted on field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SUPRA 55, Carl Zeiss). The operating voltage used in this experiment was set to 3 kV and the working distance was 2 mm. TEM images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (FEI Talos F200X, USA), the samples for TEM analysis were prepared by spreading a drop of synthesized dilute dispersion on copper grids coated with a carbon film at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by exploiting FT-IR 5700 spectrometer (USA). The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectrum was available on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo Scientific). The average particle size of the assemblies was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on the Malvern equipment. Ultraviolet–visible absorption was detected by spectrophotometer (LAMBDA 950, USA). A fluorescence test was performed on a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer, France).

Stability is one of the key factors for the biomedical applications of nano-scaled DDSs. The stability study of UC NPs and UCCu2+ NPs stored at room temperature and dispersed in ultrapure water was studied by measuring the size and zeta potential in the mixed solution daily for up to five days.

The controlled release evaluation was determined by a dialysis method. All samples were sealed in dialysis bags (Sigma, 14 000 MW cutoff) and immersed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions (pH 5.5) in the absence or presence of 10 mM GSH. The tubes were maintained at 37 °C in a water bath shaker at 130 rpm. Samples were withdrawn at predetermined time intervals (replaced with an equivalent volume of release medium) and the release amount of CDs were analyzed using UV–vis. Each controlled release analysis was repeated three times.

The disassembly of UCCu2+ NPs under GSH stimulation in a weakly acidic medium (pH = 5.5) was determined by TEM and DLS measurements. Briefly, UCCu2+ NPs (200 µg/mL) were dispersed in PB buffer (pH = 5.5) incubated with or without GSH (0.5 mM), after incubation at 37 °C (130 rpm) for 3 h, these solutions were added dropwise to the copper net and dried (60 °C, 24 h) for subsequent TEM observation. Two samples were fabricated for the DLS measurement, which contained UCCu2+ NPs (50 µg/mL) dispersed in the PB buffer (pH = 7.4, 10 mM) as the blank, and dispersed in the PB buffer (pH = 5.5) with the addition of GSH as the experimental group.

MB was introduced for the specific detection of –OH produced by UCCu2+ NPs. To be specific, UCCu2+ NPs were first dispersed in PB buffer (pH = 5.5, 10 mM) at a concentration of 15 µg/mL, followed by the addition of MB and H2O2 at a final concentration of 0.5 mM and 1 mM, respectively. The other three control groups were set as (a) MB + H2O2, (b) MB + UCCu2+ NPs, and (c) MB + H2O2 + UCCu2+ NPs. All samples were incubated in constant temperature shaking incubator at 37 °C (130 rpm) for 3 h before measuring the fluorescence emission peak at around 664 nm.

A thermal infrared imager (Fortic 323pro, China) was engaged to estimate the photothermal performance of UCCu2+ NPs. Firstly, UCCu2+ NPs (200 µg/mL) aqueous solution was placed in a disposable cuvette and irradiated with a 638 nm laser at a series of the power density (0.13–2.53 W/cm2). Then, UCCu2+ NPs with gradient concentrations (0–200 µg/mL) were loaded into cuvettes and irradiated with a 638 nm laser at fixed power density of 1 W/cm2. Finally, photothermal conversion efficiency (η) [24] was calculated according to the formula (1):

Relative parameters were specified as follows: h refers to the coefficient of heat transfer; A denoted the superficial area of the cupola; ΔT max, mix and ΔT max, water were the maximal temperature change of UCCu2+ NPs solution and ultrapure water during the irradiation process, respectively. I stands for the laser power utilized, and Aλ denoted as the absorbance of UCCu2+ NPs at 638 nm laser irradiation.

Three kinds of cell lines were involved in this study. The human hepatoma cell line HepG2, human hepatocarcinoma cell line SMMC-7721, and human being's liver cell strain L02 were purchased from the Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). The HepG2 and SMMC 7721 cell lines were cultured in DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The L02 cell line was grown in RPMI-1640 containing 10% FBS. All cells were incubated in an incubator at 37 ℃ with 5% CO2.

The cellular uptake and tracking of UCCu2+ NPs were investigated through cell imaging experiments. Briefly, HepG2 cells were medium overnight in 12-well plates until adherent and then co-incubated with UCCu2+ NPs (10 μg/mL) for 4 h. For the observation of the location of nanodrugs, the cells were incubated with LysoTracker Red (10 nM) for another 20 min, then washed, stained with Hoechst 33,342 (5 μg/mL) and fixed. Finally, the cells were photographed using a confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP8, German).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and in vitro CDT study
ce:section.ce:para:
Cytotoxicity of UCCu2+ NPs and their counterparts were detected by MTT assay. Briefly, 100 µL cells (HepG2 and L02) were seeded in 96-well plates at the concentration of 1 × 105 cells per well overnight until adhesion. Afterward, the cell culture medium was replaced with diverse concentrations of UCCu2+ NPs (0–40 µg/mL) and their counterparts for 24 h. Then, 10 µL MTT were then added into each well of plate and cultured with cells for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance was detected by a microplate reader at the wavelength of 490 nm. The cell viability (%) was determined by comparing the absorbance at 490 nm with control wells merely containing cell culture medium. The data were shown as the mean and standard error of three independent experiments. The combination index (CI) analysis was calculated based on the following formula:
(2)
CI =
C
A , x
/ I
C
x , A
+
C
B , x
/ I
C
x , B
Where CA,x, and CB,x are the concentrations of agent A and B, respectively, when achieving x% drug effect in combination treatment. ICx, A and ICx, B are single agent concentrations when it was alone used to achieve the same drug effect. There is a synergism if CI is smaller than 1.

Intracellular ROS levels were analyzed by using the fluorescent probe 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydro fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA). To be specific, HepG2 cells (1 × 105 cells/ mL) were seeded in 24-well plates all night until adherent. In brief, 10 µM DCFH-DA was diffused in DMEM culture and co-incubated for 20 min. Then washed with PBS before separating them into three groups: (1) Medium only; (2) Cultured with UCCu2+ NPs (25 µg/mL); (3) First incubated with UCCu2+ NPs (25 µg/mL) for 4 h and then exposure to 638 nm laser at a density of 1 W/cm2 for 10 min before cleaning with PBS for twice. Afterward, all samples were fixed and imaged with a confocal laser (λex = 488 nm, λem = 508–552 nm).

A calcein-AM/PI double staining kit was employed for exploring the intracellular CDT efficacy of UCCu2+ NPs. Concisely, the HepG2 cells were cultured at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and divided into four groups: (1) The blank group without any treatment; (2) The group incubated with 500 µM H2O2 only; (3) The group added with UCCu2+ NPs only; (4) The group incubated with both UCCu2+ NPs and H2O2 for 24 h, respectively. The cells were then rinsed with PBS before suspending in 500 µL of the working solution containing Calcein-AM (2 µM) and PI (5 µM) for 15 min. Finally, the sampling to be tested was observed by fluorescence microscopy.

Female ICR mice were acquired from Fuzhou Wushi Animal Center. All care and handling of animals were performed with the approval of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Fuzhou University (2021-SG-056). Each ICR mouse was subcutaneously inoculated with H22 cells suspended in 0.9% PBS saline with a density of 1 × 106. Tumor volume was calculated as follows:
(3)
Volume
m

( L e n g t h × W i d t

Blood compatibility of UCCu2+ NPs was evaluated via a hemolysis test. In brief, the blood of ICR mice was collected in the anticoagulative tube. After washing with PBS three times, the blood was 50 times diluted with PBS solution and divided into five groups: (1) Ultra-pure water (positive group); (2) PBS group (negative group); (3) CDs; (4) UC NPs and (5) UCCu2+ NPs were added into the tube loaded with blood, respectively. All samples were standing at 37 °C for 3 h and then centrifuged at 10500 rpm for 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence and photothermal imaging
ce:section.ce:para: A liver cancer model was constructed using H22 cells, and then fluorescent dyes including Cy 5.5, free CDs, and UCCu2+ NPs were intravenously injected into tumor-bearing mice through tail vein. The fluorescence signal of mice at different time points were recorded via IVIS Spectrum system (PerkinElmer, USA). The mice were then sacrificed at 24 h post-injection, and the main organs and tumor tissues were collected for ex vivo imaging (λex = 638 nm). For photothermal imaging, tumor-bearing mice were separated into three groups, PBS (100 µL), CDs (100 µL), and UCCu2+ NPs (100 µL, with equal UA NPs concentration of 15 mg/kg) were injected into mice of each group, respectively. At 4 h post-injection, tumors of mice were exposed to a 638 nm laser irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 5 min, the thermal images and intratumoral temperature changes every 30 s were recorded using the thermal infrared imager.

To estimate the anticancer activity, the 25 H22 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into five groups and received different treatments: (1) The group injected with PBS; (2) The group injected with UA NPs; (3) The group injected with CDs and exposed to 638 nm laser irradiation; (4) The group injected with UC NPs and exposed to 638 nm laser irradiation; (5) The group injected with UCCu2+ NPs and exposed to 638 nm laser irradiation. The laser irradiation was conducted at 4 h post-injection with a density of 1 W/cm2 for 10 min. Besides, the tumor volume and mouce body weight were recorded every other day for seven cycles.

In vivo toxicity of UCCu2+ NPs and the changes of tumor tissue in different groups after treatment was investigated by the histology staining assay. Mice were sacrificed at day 14 after post-administration for harvest of the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney). Finally, the tissues were gathered for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain.

Statistical analysis was performed using Student’s t-test. All data shown in this article were expressed as the mean ± SD for at least three separate experiments; differences with P＜0.05 were considered significant (*). Statistical differences were defined as *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of UCCu2+ NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
A schematic of the fabrication process of UCCu2+ NPs was presented in Scheme 1 A. The CDs introduced acted as both a fluorescence imaging and phototherapy agent [15,33]. The UA was subjected to chemotherapy analysis and the Cu2+ proposed acted as glue for assembling and endowed UCCu2+ NPs with CDT and GSH consumption function in TME. To prepare the favorable nanoassemblies and improve the encapsulation efficiency of the DDSs, the manufacturing process and adding ratio of UA, CDs, and Cu2+ added were optimized. To obtain the optimum formulation of UA NPs, different concentrations of UA NPs were prepared. As displayed in Fig. S1
C, when the mass content of UA (4 mg/mL) was 50 µL, the suspension was completely dissolved to form a solution. Furthermore, at this concentration, the size distribution (Fig. S1 A) and SEM analysis ( Fig. S2 ) confirmed a uniform distribution with a diameter of approximately 150.5 ± 2.3 nm at such concentration, which was selected for subsequent use. After assembling with well-distributed spherical CDs with an average diameter of 4.46 nm ( Fig. S3 ) as described in our previously reported method through hydrogen bond interaction [24], the obtained UC NPs with different mass ratios (20:1, 10:1, 5:1 to 1:1 and 1:5, 1:10, to 1:20) of UA NPs to CDs were also investigated. According to the size distribution and SEM morphology analysis (Fig. S4 A and C), the mass ratio (10:1) of UA NPs to CDs was selected for subsequent use. Next, the drug-loading content (DLC) was compared. As shown in Table S1 , when the mass ratio of UA NPs to CDs was 10:1, based on the standard curve, the DLC value of CDs in the prepared UC NPs was 52.5 ± 0.6% ( Fig. S5 ), which was higher than that prepared at other ratios. To fabricate the Cu2+ induced UCCu2+ NPs, an optimal of 0.5 mM Cu2+ was screened by particle size ( Fig. S6 ), zeta potential, and morphology (SEM analysis).

The particle size change induced by the formation of nanoassemblies was analyzed by DLS measurement. As illustrated in Fig. 1
A, the particle size of UCCu2+ NPs was obviously increased to 188.9 ± 1.9 nm, indicating the assembly has a suitable particle size to facilitate their accumulation in the tumor site. Moreover, the autocorrelation curve (Fig. S7 A) showed that the curve of UC NPs decayed faster than that of UCCu2+ NPs, and the obtained autocorrelation time was shorter, indicating that the particle size of UC NPs is smaller than that of UCCu2+ NPs. The fitting model used to analyze the autocorrelation curve was also shown in Fig. S7 B. Further, the surface charge of UC NPs was decreased in comparison with UA NPs (from −18.8 ± 0.6 mV to –22.3 ± 3.5), ascribing to the dipole–dipole attraction which takes place between a H-atom attached to an electronegative atom and an electronegative atom with lone pair of electrons present in the vicinity, which produces the hydrogen bonding force. Interestingly, the nanostructures can still be stably formed when the composition of UA NPs and CDs varied. For example, we studied the ratio of UA NPs to CDs from 20:1 to 1:20 ( Table S2 ), and the results showed that when the concentration of CDs increased, the zeta potential of nanoparticles formed by self-assembly with UA NPs was lower than that of free CDs, inferring the successful absorption of CDs on the UA NPs, covering on the surface of UA NPs. Whereas the zeta potential turned again due to the introduction of Cu2+ ions to form UCCu2+ NPs (1.8 ± 0.1 mV) (Fig. 1 B). This is attributed to the fact that the carboxyl group in the molecular structure of UA and citric acid is a strong coordination group, which can coordinate with the transition metal ion Cu2+ to assemble into nanoparticles. Similarly, we also investigated the effect of Cu2+ content on UCCu2+ NPs, the results ( Tables S3 ) showed that the zeta potential of UCCu2+ NPs gradually increased with the increase of copper ion content, which preliminary indicated the carboxyl group or hydroxyl group of UC NPs was coordinated with Cu2+. The positive charge of NPs in the TME led to a more efficient cellular uptake by tumor cells, which showed a better delivery efficiency in vivo by charge reversal. Furthermore, the high-angle ring dark fields (HAADF) and elemental mappings of UCCu2+ NPs, C, O, N, and Cu were evenly distributed (Fig. 1 E). As shown in Fig. 1 E, the particle size of UCCu2+ NPs was measured to be approximately 195.4 ± 1.2 nm. The increase in particle size of UCCu2+ NPs relative to UC NPs (136 ± 0.8 nm) (Fig. 1 C and D) may be due to the successful introduction of Cu2+ ions, resulting in the stability of the final structure, which further confirmed the formation of UCCu2+ NPs spheres. The observed slightly larger particle size from DLS than that of from TEM are generally attributed to the formation of hydration layers on the surface of UCCu2+ NPs. Subsequently, the surface conditions and chemical compositions of these materials were further analyzed by FTIR and XPS analysis. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 F), CDs appeared obvious peaks at about 750, 1110, 1620, and 2360 cm−1 and a shoulder peak at about 1740 cm−1, ascribing to the NH, CN, CC, O-CO, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, confirming the presence of amino (–NH2) and carboxyl (–COOH) [34]. Compared with the UA NPs alone, the FTIR spectra of UC NPs showed an additional peak at about 1560 cm−1, attributing to the NH bending vibration of the amino, which further indicated the successful assembling of CDs on the UA NPs [35]. Moreover, a newly emerged peak at about 1110 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of UCCu2+ NPs was derived from the stretching vibration of the metal–ligand bond [17,36], demonstrating the coordination between Cu2+ and UC NPs. The XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ) further confirmed their chemical compositions. Particularly, the survey spectrum of UCCu2+ NPs (Fig. 2 I-L) exhibited the distinctive peaks of O 1 s (531.2 eV), C 1 s (284.6 eV), and N 1 s (399.8 eV) were divided from the deconvoluted peaks and indicated the existence of UA NPs (Fig. 2 A-B) and CDs (Fig. 2 C-E), which revealed potentials of the assemblies for laser mediated phototherapy and imaging capabilities, while the Cu 2p has two dominant peaks at 933.9 and 952.6e V, assigning to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [37]. Therefore, the existence of Cu2+ in UCCu2+ NPs has been verified again.

Moreover, the excitation-dependent photoluminescence phenomenon was observed in the UC NPs, as the excitation wavelength increased within a certain range (360–550 nm) with an interval of 10, the corresponding emission wave increased first and then decreased (Fig. 1 G), which was ascribed to the definite sizes of CDs, the optical selection of particle surface defects and N band gap, proving the CDs were successfully assembled into UC NPs [38]. Interestingly, the fluorescence of UC NPs at the optimal emission channel of CDs (λex = 490 nm) was sharply quenched when assembled into UCCu2+ NPs (Fig. 1 H), which was mainly attributed to the Cu2+ induced aggregation of UC NPs and electron/energy transfer process [39,40]. These phenomenon demonstrated that the fluorescence function and imaging characteristics of CDs in the nanoassemblies were in quenched state under neutral condition. Then, the storage stability of nanoassemblies was further verified. As shown in Fig. 3
A and B, no significant changes were observed in both size and zeta potential of UCCu2+ NPs during storage for up to 5 days. Moreover, UCCu2+ NPs still maintained favorable colloidal stability (Fig. 3 C) even in two mediums containing 10% FBS: RPMI-1640 and DMEM, respectively, demonstrating UCCu2+ NPs with excellent stability in a dispersed state.

Subsequently, the in vitro release profile of CDs was monitored at different reductive triggers (with and without the addition of GSH). The release curve showed that only about 29% of cumulative CDs were released from UCCu2+ NPs in the absence of GSH at pH 5.5. In contrast, the release of CDs was significantly accelerated after treatment with GSH as high as 10 mM (a biologically relevant level of 2–10 mM) at pH 5.5 [41], and a passive release plateau appeared within 12 h. Importantly, the final cumulative release ratio of CDs culminated was approximately 58% at pH 5.5 in the presence of 10 mM GSH. Whereas the drug cumulative release proportion of free CDs was as high as 72% during the 12 h release period in the absence of GSH at pH 5.5. In addition, the introduction of 10 mM GSH only slightly increased free CDs release compared to that without GSH as presented in Fig. 3 D. These results demonstrate that UCCu2+ NPs responded to reduction-triggered activation, achieving the on-demand release of drugs.

As a therapeutic agent designed for specific anticancer, it is particularly important to evaluate the stimulus response of UCCu2+ NPs towards TME such as lower pH, overexpressed GSH, and supernormal H2O2. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence emission of CDs from UCCu2+ NPs was observed to be gradually recovered as the GSH concentration increased in an acidic environment (pH = 5.5), which may be ascribed to the dissociation of UCCu2+ NPs under the stimulation of acidic and reductive dual-sensitive triggers (pH 5.5 and 10 mM GSH), resulting in fluorescence recovery, which was confirmed by TEM measurement ( Fig. S8 ), verifying the stimulus-responsive disassembly properties of UCCu2+ NPs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4 B, the Cu2+ can be reduced to Cu+ by GSH, and meanwhile the electrostatic repulsion between H+ and Cu2+ ions will be enhanced in acidic medium, which will weaken the coordination ability between Cu2+ and UC NPs, thus contributing to the disassembly of UCCu2+ NPs. In addition, the change in the GSH ratio detected by the commercial kit further proved that Cu2+ participated in the redox reaction of GSH and also caused the consumption of GSH. As depicted in Fig. 4 C, the GSH ratio after incubation with UCCu2+ NPs was nearly half that of the blank group, inferring a significant GSH depletion capacity of UCCu2+ NPs. It is acknowledged that the GSH is a scavenger of ROS in biosystem, therefore the study of this property has far-reaching significance for improving ROS-based cancer treatment efficiency. Additionally, previous studies have shown that Cu2+ ions can effectively catalyze H2O2 to produce –OH even under mildly acidic conditions through a Fenton-like reaction (Fig. 4 B) [42]. The –OH generation capacity of UCCu2+ NPs can be determined using MB with a typical absorption peak at about 667 nm as a fluorescence probe (that is, the MB can degrade –OH generated by a Fenton-like reaction between Cu2+ ions and H2O2, leading to weakened fluorescence). As depicted in Fig. 4 D, the fluorescence intensity from MB, H2O2, and UCCu2+ NPs mixture was found to be significantly weakened, which verified the efficient H2O2-responsive generation of –OH and consequent CDT capacity of UCCu2+ NPs. Therefore, the lower pH, supernormal H2O2, and overexpressed GSH in TME can trigger multiple funcitons of UCCu2+ NPs, such as fluorescence recovery, particle disassembly, GSH depletion, and –OH generation, showing great potential as intelligent cancer theranostic agents.

Given the broad absorption of UCCu2+ NPs, their feasibility acting as a PTT agent was further investigated. Firstly, we studied the laser dose-dependent temperature alteration of UCCu2+ NPs at a fixed concentration of 200 µg/mL. As revealed in Fig. 5
A, the temperature of UCCu2+ NPs increased quickly with the increase of laser power (0.13–2.53 W/cm2). Previous studies have shown that the cancer cells can be destroyed within minutes when the temperature is higher than 50 °C [43], and 1 W/cm2 was employed for the following experiments. Secondly, the temperature elevation of UCCu2+ NPs were evaluated under a concentration gradient (0–200 µg/mL). As exhibited in Fig. 5 B, upon 638 nm laser irradiation, temperatures elevation of the UCCu2+ NPs quickly rise and finally reached to 12.6 °C to 63.6 °C with the concentrations from 25 to 200 µg/mL at 1 W/cm2 for 10 min, whereas only a 2.6 °C increment was observed in the control group. Furthermore, to calculate the η of UCCu2+ NPs, the temperature changes of UCCu2+ NPs dispersion (100 µg/mL) as a function of time was recorded (Fig. 5 D) according to the reported method [44]. By using the fitting parameters of the cooling stage in Fig. 5 E, η of UCCu2+ NPs was calculated to be 42%. These results manifested excellent photothermal property of UCCu2+ NPs and which can be applied as a suitable PTT agent. Notably, compared with the mixture of UCCu2+ NPs and MB (–OH indicator) without laser irradiation, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly after laser irradiation (Fig. 5 C), indicating the thermal amplification effect induced by UCCu2+ NPs produced –OH. Subsequently, the photosensitization property of UCCu2+ NPs was detected by ROS scavenger DPBF. As revealed in Fig. 5 F, the mixture of UCCu2+ NPs and DPBF has an obvious absorption peak at 415 nm, corresponding to DPBF. However, significant degradation of DPBF was observed after 5 min of 638 nm laser irradiation, inferring the NPs a suitable PDT agent. More interestingly, significantly weakened fluorescence emission from DPBF in UCCu2+ NPs dispersion were observed in the presence of GSH than that of without GSH under 638 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2) compared with the group without laser irradiation, demonstrating more ROS was generated in the former case. The higher photosensitization performance of UCCu2+ NPs in the presence of GSH might be due to their disassembly and lower quenched Cu+ that caused by GSH. These results are attractive because this means that the PDT function of UCCu2+ NPs will remain quenched under neutral conditions, thereby reducing its photocytotoxicity. It is worth noting that the GSH consumption potency of UCCu2+ NPs can not only promote ROS generation but also the production of –OH through a Fenton-like reaction, which ultimately enhanced the ROS-relevant therapeutic efficacy. Altogether, the diverse stimulus–response characteristics of UCCu2+ NPs endowed them with the potential to realize fluorescence imaging-guided synergetic CDT, PTT, and PDT in cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic therapy and intracellular trafficking of UCCu2+ NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the unique TME stimuli-responsive properties of UCCu2+ NPs, the practicability for fluorescence imaging and synergetic therapy was studied at the cellular level. Fluorescence imaging of UCCu2+ NPs towards HepG2 cells was examined via confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). As exhibited in Fig. S9 , the HepG2 cells have prominent red emissions that are easily distinguished, indicating that UCCu2+ NPs have effective cell penetration and TME stimuli-responsive fluorescence recovery. Here, the Hoechst was utilized to target the nucleus, further confirming the comparative staining compatibility of the nanoassemblies.

For in vitro CDT, the antiproliferative actions of UCCu2+ NPs and their counterparts were first examined in carcinoma cell lines (HepG2 cells) and normal cell lines (L02 cells) using the MTT assay. Fig. 6
B showed that even if the concentration of UCCu2+ NPs was as high as 20 μg/mL, the survival rate of L02 cells remained above 80%. On the contrary, the survival rate of HepG2 cells decreased rapidly with an increased concentration of UCCu2+ NPs. About 20% of HepG2 cells remained alive after incubating with UCCu2+ NPs at 40 μg/mL (Fig. 6 A), thereby demonstrating that the cytotoxicity of nanoassemblies towards carcinoma cell was higher than that of normal cells. Specifically, HepG2 cells cultured with CDs solution showed no significant cytotoxicity without laser irradiation. However, the cell viability of HepG2 cells decreased rapidly with an increasing concentration of CDs upon 638 nm laser irradiation. To further test the antitumor efficiency of UC NPs in vitro, the cytotoxicity of UC NPs in the dark or upon laser irradiation was evaluated. The data showed that the antitumor efficiency of UA NPs was not affected by CDs, as indicated by the similar dark cytotoxicity of UA NPs and UC NPs. In contrast, UC NPs under laser irradiation achieved a favorable tumor-killing effect. The calculated combination index [24] (CI < 1) indicated synergism when combining phototherapy (CDs) and chemotherapy (UA NPs) ( Fig. S10
A). Moreover, the cell viability of HepG2 cells incubated with UCCu2+ NPs upon laser irradiation rapidly decreased, which can be mainly attributed to the promoted –OH production through a Fenton-like reaction and GSH consumption under specific TME conditions. Furthermore, a lower cytotoxicity of UC NPs was observed in the HepG2 cells, thus confirming the CDT efficacy of UCCu2+ NPs resulted from the contained Cu2+, showing that UCCu2+ NPs had the best efficiency in killing tumor cells. In addition, the calculated CI＜1 indicated synergism when combining UC NPs and Cu2+ ( Fig. S10
B). The enhanced antitumor efficacy was further confirmed by Annexin V-FITC and PI co-staining. As presented in Fig. 6 C, UCCu2+ NPs had the best antiproliferative activity against HepG2 cells when compared with their counterparts, which contributed to its synergistic effect. Thus the above-mentioned results indicated that UCCu2+ NPs and their counterparts could induce apoptosis of HepG2 cells to varying degrees when compared with medium only. The apoptosis rates of HepG2 cells treated with UA NPs, CDs, CDs irradiated with laser, UC NPs, UC NPs irradiated with laser, UCCu2+ NPs, and UCCu2+ NPs irradiated with laser were 23.40%, 8.88%, 25.83%, 27.4%, 38.64%, 50.2%, and 68.1%, respectively. Furthermore, the apoptotic bodies experiment also verified the collaborative reciprocity of UCCu2+ NPs towards carcinoma cells (HepG2 and SMMC 7721) ( Fig. S11 ). To further verify the detailed efficacy of UCCu2+ NPs on CDT, Calcein-AM/PI double staining assays were performed to further verify the detailed efficacy of UCCu2+ NPs on CDT. As depicted in Fig. 6 E, the cellular distribution of live (Green) and dead (Red) HepG2 cells in UCCu2+ NPs and medium-only-treated groups was consistent with the cytotoxicity results. The vast majority of HepG2 cells cultured in combination with UCCu2+ NPs and H2O2 was killed compared to cells treated with H2O2 alone, thereby confirming that H2O2-triggers CDT (Fig. 6 E). Moreover, reports on the favorable character of ROS in CDs-induced apoptosis inspired us to utilize ROS probes (DCFH-DA) to further investigate intracellular ROS levels [45]. As seen in Fig. 6 D, the confocal microscopy showed a brighter green fluorescence in UCCu2+ NPs treated cells, and Pearson’s correlation coefficient quantization further confirmed the increased ROS production, indicating that UCCu2+ NPs had superior anticancer activity. Overall, UCCu2+ NPs exhibited TME stimuli-responsive fluorescence imaging and synergistic antitumor properties of CDT, PTT, and PDT with favorable in vitro selectivity.

Following uptake, intracellular trafficking of nanodrugs is another key process that determines their anticancer activities. As exhibited in Fig. 7, UCCu2+ NPs-treated HepG2 cells showed co-localization (yellow region) of lysosomes (red fluorescence) and UCCu2+ NPs (green fluorescence), which indicated that most of the intake UCCu2+ NPs was localized in the lysosome. Importantly, co-localization fluorescence increased first and then decreased with the increase of incubation time (1, 2, 4, and 8 h), and reached a peak at 2 h post-incubation, which indicated that UCCu2+ NPs had efficient endo/lysosome escape abilities. In summary, the highly efficient endo/lysosome escape capability and nuclear-targeting talent of UCCu2+ NPs are the reasons for their good cell-killing activity.

Inspired by the excellent TME-triggered fluorescence imaging and synergetic therapy performance of UCCu2+ NPs at cellular level, their antitumor effect and imaging capacity in vivo were further explored. Firstly, to ensure the biosafety of UCCu2+ NPs in antitumor applications, the blood compatibility of UCCu2+ NPs was further evaluated by the hemolysis test. As displayed in Fig. S12 , UCCu2+ NPs, and their counterparts showed no significant erythrocyte color even at 40 μg/mL compared to the positive control group, implying high blood compatibility of the nanoassemblies. Then, the in vivo distributions of Cy5.5, free CDs, and UCCu2+ NPs was measured using IVIS spectroscopic imaging system. As presented in Fig. 8
A, compared with the Cy 5.5-treated group with a random distribution of fluorescence throughout the body, the group injected with UCCu2+ NPs showed a stronger fluorescence signal at the tumor site 1 h post-injection and remained at a higher level till the end of the observation period (24 h), a valley of fluorescence signals appeared at the tumor site 4 h post-administration, which was confirmed by the quantitative analysis (Fig. 8 D). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in tumors recessed quickly and almost disappeared 8 h post-injection, which manifested the longer retention ability of the NPs. Moreover, the ex vivo fluorescence imaging also proclaimed the enhanced accumulation of UCCu2+ NPs (Fig. 8 B), which predicted the passive targeting of UCCu2+ NPs by EPR effects. Most importantly, the NPs were primarily distributed in the liver and kidney (Fig. 8 C), suggesting UCCu2+ NPs could be cleaned out of mice body through both renal and hepatic pathways. To sum up, all in vivo distribution outcomes signified that UCCu2+ NPs can function as excellent antitumor agents with improved tumor accumulation and retention.

According to the bio-distribution results, the photothermal performance of UCCu2+ NPs was examined by an infrared thermal imager at the optimal time point of 4 h post administration. As exhibited in Fig. 9
A, UCCu2+ NPs injected mice exhibited the maximal temperature elevation at tumor site from 31.9 °C to 54.3 °C upon 638 nm laser irradiation at 1 W/cm2 for 5 min, while the temperatures at tumor sites of PBS and CDs injected groups were 39.9 °C and 47.1 °C, respectively. Even more, the tumor temperature of UCCu2+ NPs-treated group was observed to elevate much faster than that of the PBS and CDs-administrated groups (Fig. 9 B). Implying the reinforced accumulation capacity and PTT efficacy of UCCu2+ NPs in vivo ascribed to the larger diameter size.

Motivated by the favorable homing capacity of the tumor, UCCu2+ NPs played an irreplaceable role in synergetic therapy. The tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups at random and performed various treatments (1). Blank group (PBS), (2). Chemotherapy group (UA NPs), (3). PTT + PDT group (CDs + laser), (4). Chemotherapy + PDT + PTT (UC NP + Laser), (5). Chemotherapy + PDT + PTT + CDT (UCCu2+ NPs + Laser). All nanodrugs were administrated via Caudal injection with equal concentrations of 15 mg/kg of UA NPs (100 μL) and 1.5 mg/kg of CDs. Tumor volume and body weight of mice were then recorded regularly the implement of five group treatments. The tumor inhibition rate of UA NPs was significantly lower than that of UCCu2+ NPs + laser irradiation group, which exhibited the highest tumor inhibition efficacy even slightly shrinking about 89%, which was due to the CDT effect of UCCu2+ NPs, implying the considerable antitumor effect of PTT combined PDT, verifying the supreme effects through the trimodal therapy (Fig. 9 F). Despite tumor recurrence was observed in almost all groups, only mice in UCCu2+ NPs-injected group showed the highest tumor inhibition efficiency. The photographs of the tumor mass (Fig. 9 C) and the average weight of tumors (Fig. 9 D) after various treatments were consistent with the above results. In addition, no obvious weight loss in the mice was observed during the 7-cycle treatments (Fig. 9 E).

Furthermore, to analyze the tumor damage effect induced by different treatments, tumors from diverse groups were excised for H&E staining at the end of the monitoring period (Fig. 9). Compared with the blank group, it was found that the CDT + PTT + PDT group had no obvious inflammation and injury, which further indicated that UCCu2+ NPs had lower toxicity in vivo. These results suggested the great potentials of UCCu2+ NPs to serve as a promising synergistic antitumor agent with negligible side effects.

In this study, a smart HCC theranostic system was fabricated via the assembly of chemotherapeutic agent UA NPs, CDs PSs, and Cu2+, which exhibited remarkable features of TME stimuli-responsive fluorescence imaging and synergistic therapy by chemotherapy, phototherapy (including PTT and PDT), and heat-reinforced CDT. The data showed that the introduction of Cu2+ not only promoted lysosomal escape, but also played a vital role in CDT function due to their reaction with H2O2 to form –OH. Most importantly, the contained Cu2+ realized efficient GSH depletion in tumors, leading to amplification of intracellular oxidative stress and increased efficiency of ROS-related therapy. Activation of fluorescence imaging and ROS- related therapy were observed in vitro with enhanced treatment outcomes only towards carcinoma cells. The in vivo results confirmed enhanced accumulation abilities of UCCu2+ NPs at the tumor sites and an exceptional anticancer effect with low systemic toxicity. Compared with traditional therapy using chemotherapy alone with side effects and MDR [46–48], in this study, multiple therapies were integrated into one to achieve a three-pronged collaborative treatment, thereby overcoming the disadvantages of traditional treatment. The key improvements in this study were that UCCu2+ NPs solved the problem of small size limitation of CDs for their applications in cancer biomedicine as well as developed a fluorescence imaging guided cancer synergistic treatment agent that could effectively enhance therapeutic efficacy with minimized side effects by fully taking advantage of the TME. This may open an avenue and give the inspiration to construct novel theranostics and hold promise for achieving enhanced therapeutic efficacy with minimized adverse effects.

Chun-Mei Lai: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jia Xu: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology. Bing-Chen Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Dong-Miao Li: Resources. Jiang-Wen Shen: Data curation. Shi-Jing Yu: Resources. Jing-Wei Shao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
